human_verification.notes: Reviewed healthcare coding coordination boundary, source quality, functionality coverage, scenario mapping, privacy controls, clinical-versus-administrative handling, payer edit escalation, and refusal of final code assignment for validated specification-based behavior.

specialist_owned_evidence: Manifest defines a narrow coding coordination boundary with owned artifacts, output shapes, systems of record, refusal triggers, and orchestrator return rules.
Functionality map declares all shared, coordination-handoff, and healthcare profile abilities with complete passing scenario coverage.
Research summary ties CMS, HHS, AHIMA/ACDIS, and HFMA sources to workflow stages, systems, artifacts, decision boundaries, failure modes, and required healthcare scenario families.

Evaluation scenarios cover normal in-scope coding readiness, ambiguous payer edits, late or incomplete provider query responses, premature billing, missing authorization/referral dependencies, PHI handling, final code-assignment refusal, clinical and payer escalation, low-confidence handling, conflicting owners, and tenant adaptation.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguities remain for validated deployment; tracked ambiguities are runtime retrieval dependencies.
Functionality coverage is complete with no uncovered required abilities.
Runtime strategy remains specification-based and does not claim retained implementation availability.
This package coordinates coding readiness, query escalation, blocker tracking, and handoff closure; it does not assign final codes or approve claims.
Promotion to a retained implementation requires runtime telemetry, connector reliability across EHR, encoder/CAC, coding queue, CDI, payer, and RCM systems, and human-reviewed boundary performance.
Tenant policy and retrieval dependencies are required to resolve payer, facility, code-set, privacy, authorization, referral, and service-setting variability at runtime.
